2024 · IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence · Benchmarks & Evals

Inadequacies of Large Language Model Benchmarks in the Era of Generative Artificial Intelligence

A People-Process-Technology audit of 23 widely-used LLM benchmarks — from MMLU to AgentBench — finds every single one carries at least one unresolved inadequacy, and two issues (response variability, and telling genuine reasoning apart from benchmark-specific optimization) affect 22 of the 23.

Timothy R. McIntosh, Teo Sušnjak, Nalin A. G. Arachchilage, Tong Liu, Dan Xu, Paul A. Watters, Malka N. Halgamuge
venue IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence citations 130 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By early 2024 the field had over 700,000 LLMs listed on HuggingFace and no shared standard for comparing them — unlike aviation or automotive testing, where regulation and public consensus fix what a "benchmark" has to demonstrate. Into that vacuum, researchers kept minting their own LLM benchmarks, each with its own tasks, metrics, and (often unstated) assumptions about what counts as a valid test. The authors' complaint isn't that any single benchmark is bad; it's that the term "benchmark" had become "applied more liberally in academic publishing than warranted" — a self-declared label attached to test sets that were never checked for two properties the authors argue are non-negotiable: functionality (does the benchmark actually measure the capability it claims to?) and integrity (can a model game it — through memorization, overfitting, or surface-pattern exploitation — and still score well?).

Existing critiques of LLM evaluation tended to look at one slice at a time — a paper on prompt sensitivity, a paper on contamination, a paper on cultural bias — without a shared framework for triaging which of those problems apply to which benchmarks and how badly. And of the 23 benchmarks the authors eventually settle on for their audit, only 6 (later stated elsewhere in the same paper as 5 — more on that discrepancy below) had passed peer review at the time of writing, meaning most of the field's yardsticks were unrefereed preprints. The gap this paper targets is a systematic, side-by-side audit: take a broad, representative slice of the benchmarks the community was actually using in 2023, and run every one of them through the same checklist of functionality and integrity failure modes, rather than critiquing benchmarks piecemeal or in isolation.

// how it works

The method

The authors borrow the People, Process, Technology (PPT) framework from cybersecurity risk assessment as an organizing lens: a benchmark's trustworthiness depends on who builds and judges it (People — the diversity and expertise of developers and evaluators), how consistently it's constructed and run (Process — standardization, prompt engineering, iteration speed), and what its technical infrastructure can and can't measure (Technology — response variability, reasoning vs. memorization, installation cost). Crossed with the paper's two core criteria, functionality and integrity, this yields eleven concrete "inadequacy" categories — six technological, three processual, two human — each defined narrowly enough to check off against a real benchmark.

Candidate benchmarks came from a structured literature review of peer-reviewed publications, preprints, and widely-cited platforms up to October 2023, filtered for benchmarks explicitly designed to evaluate LLMs with substantial academic or technical adoption. That produced 23 benchmarks spanning general-purpose suites (MMLU, BIG-bench, HELM, AGIEval, Chain-of-Thought Hub, KoLA, ARB, Xiezhi, C-Eval, PromptBench) and domain-specific ones (HumanEval for code, LegalBench for law, MultiMedQA for medicine, FLUE for finance, plus a cluster of tool-use and agentic benchmarks — ToolAlpaca, ToolBench, AgentBench, APIBank, BOLAA — and HaluEval for hallucination).

Each benchmark was then walked through the flowchart in Fig. 1 for every one of the eleven inadequacy categories: does the inadequacy show up in this benchmark at all? If so, did the original authors address it? If not addressed, did they at least acknowledge it? The result is a three-way code per benchmark-category pair — ✓ (present, unacknowledged), △ (acknowledged but unresolved), or × (considered addressed, or not present) — tabulated across all 23 benchmarks in the paper's Table II. From that grid the authors define a single summary statistic:

prevalence = benchmarks scored ✓ or △ on inadequacy k23 // "the number of benchmarks (out of 23) exhibiting each specific inadequacy" — Sec. III-D
// what they found

The results

Every one of the 23 benchmarks was flagged with at least one unaddressed or under-addressed inadequacy. Two issues were nearly universal: response variability under standardized evaluation and the inability to distinguish genuine reasoning from technical optimization each hit 22 of 23 benchmarks. The response-variability finding has a concrete illustration the authors cite: reformatting multiple-choice indicators from "(A)" to "[A]," or adding extra whitespace, has been shown to shift measured accuracy by roughly 5% — a purely cosmetic input change moving the scoreboard. At the other end, the least-prevalent issue was benchmarks using LLMs to generate or judge their own evaluation data (9 of 23) — still true of M3KE, which used ChatGPT-generated multiple-choice questions to test Chinese-language models, baking ChatGPT's own biases into the test.

No benchmark came away clean, but some fared far better than others. HaluEval (hallucination detection) was the only benchmark with zero "present-and-unacknowledged" (✓) marks across all eleven categories — its four flagged issues were all at least acknowledged (△). BOLAA (agent orchestration) came close behind, with a single unacknowledged issue. At the other extreme, FLUE (financial sentiment), M3KE (multi-subject Chinese-language knowledge), and AgentBench (agent performance) each racked up eight or more unacknowledged inadequacies out of eleven, despite testing otherwise unrelated capabilities. Installation and scalability cost was also concretely illustrated: APIBank's benchmark required integrating 73 real-world APIs, a scale of engineering effort the authors argue works against broad, consistent reproduction.

Inadequacy (by domain)Prevalence
Response Variability in Standardized Evaluations22 / 23
Genuine Reasoning vs Technical Optimization22 / 23
Tension Between Helpfulness and Harmlessness19 / 23
Linguistic Variability and Embedded Logic Diversity17 / 23
Benchmark Installation and Scalability16 / 23
Biases in LLM-Generated LLM Evaluations9 / 23
Inconsistent Benchmark Implementation18 / 23
Slow Test Iteration Time18 / 23
Challenge of Proper Prompt Engineering14 / 23
Diversity in Human Curators and Evaluators19 / 23
Diverse Cultural, Social, Political, Religious & Ideological Norms18 / 23
// does it hold up?

The eval read

Judge this as a critique essay, not an experiment: the ✓/△/× grid in Table II isn't measured from any run against a model, it's a documentation review — did the original benchmark paper mention and resolve each issue? That distinction matters because the coding is entirely the work of the seven co-authors, and Section III-D never describes a second, independent coder, an inter-rater agreement statistic, or a blinded review pass. The Limitations section (VIII-F) says this outright: "our analysis inherently carries our subjective bias," and where the team "could not conclusively determine whether a study had acknowledged or addressed a specific benchmark inadequacy... we opted to give these studies the benefit of the doubt." That's an honest disclosure, but it means every prevalence number in Fig. 2–4 is a single, unreplicated team's judgment call — precisely the kind of unvalidated coding process that later benchmark-audit papers (BetterBench's dual-reviewer-plus-tiebreaker protocol, or Bean et al.'s 29-reviewer construct-validity review with a reported Brennan–Prediger kappa) treat as a baseline requirement rather than an optional nicety.

The paper also trips over its own headline statistic. Section IV-B states "only 6 of the 23 surveyed benchmark studies... were peer-reviewed at the time of this article," but the Limitations section (VIII-F) later says "only 5 out of the 23 surveyed benchmark studies were peer-reviewed" — a small, internal 6-vs-5 inconsistency, but a pointed one for a paper whose entire thesis is that the field needs more rigor and consistency than it currently gets.

There's also a scope mismatch worth flagging in the coding itself: HumanEval — a benchmark whose stated purpose is functional correctness of generated code via unit tests, nothing more — is marked ✓ (present, unacknowledged) on "Tension Between Helpfulness and Harmlessness." It's not obvious a docstring-to-function benchmark should be expected to grapple with helpfulness-vs-harmlessness at all; applying all eleven categories uniformly to every benchmark regardless of its own declared scope risks exactly the kind of construct-mismatch the paper accuses the benchmarks themselves of. And because the review explicitly stopped at documentation ("our decision not to attempt reproducing results from the surveyed benchmark studies"), none of the integrity claims — gaming, memorization, overfitting — are empirically demonstrated against an actual model; they're argued from plausibility and cited precedent, not measured.

None of this undercuts the paper's larger, well-supported point: a late-2023 cross-section of 23 heavily-cited LLM benchmarks really does skew toward English, MCQ-format, single-turn, unreplicated preprints, and the response-variability and reasoning-vs-optimization findings (22/23 each) have aged well against what LiveBench, contamination-detection studies, and the agentic-benchmark wave later confirmed independently. But the October-2023 literature cutoff also means all 23 benchmarks predate the agentic-environment, RL-verified, and LLM-judge/Arena-style evaluation paradigms that came to dominate 2024–2025 — so a reader should treat this as an audit of first-generation exam-style LLM benchmarks specifically, not of "LLM benchmarks" as the field looks today.

baselines

There is no experimental baseline — this is a documentation review of 23 benchmarks' own papers, coded by the seven authors with no stated second-coder or inter-rater statistic.

contamination

Contamination and benchmark-gaming are the paper's central concern (the 22/23 "genuine reasoning vs. technical optimization" finding) but are argued from cited precedent, never tested empirically against a model.

what's missed

A pre-October-2023 cutoff excludes every agentic-environment, RL-verified, and LLM-as-judge/Arena-style benchmark that came to dominate the field within a year; the same 11 categories are applied uniformly regardless of a benchmark's own declared scope (e.g., dinging a pure code-correctness suite for not resolving helpfulness-vs-harmlessness).

would raise confidence

A reported inter-rater reliability statistic for the ✓/△/× coding, and an internally consistent peer-review count — Section IV-B says 6 of 23 were peer-reviewed; the Limitations section says 5.

// bottom line

The diagnosis is largely right and has held up well with hindsight; the instrument that produced the headline numbers is a single team's unreplicated documentation review — exactly the kind of unvalidated evaluation process the paper's own framework would flag in someone else's benchmark.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Applying a People-Process-Technology audit across 23 late-2023-era LLM benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, HELM, AgentBench, and 19 others), the authors find at least one unresolved inadequacy in every single one, with response variability and genuine-reasoning-vs-optimization each present in 22 of 23.
  2. Only "6" (Sec. IV-B) or "5" (Sec. VIII-F — the paper disagrees with itself) of the 23 surveyed benchmark studies had been peer-reviewed at the time of the critique, underscoring how young and unrefereed the 2021–2023 LLM-benchmark literature still was.
  3. The coding itself is a single-team, non-blinded documentation review with no reported inter-rater statistic — the same category of methodological gap the paper flags in the benchmarks it critiques — and its proposed fix (behavioral profiling plus post-deployment audits) is left as unimplemented future work, not something the authors built or tested.
